ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to Baijiu exists; closest defensible parent class is 'distilled alcoholic beverage' (FOODON_03301263).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0GJ covers distilled spirits broadly; no Baijiu-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code specifically for Baijiu identified; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Baijiu is approximately 25 ml (about 20 g), typically consumed from a small thimble-sized glass at banquets or social gatherings.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Alcoholic beverage, distilled, all (gin, rum, vodka, whiskey) 80 proof
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Baijiu is not listed individually in USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for distilled spirits (80 proof, ~40% ABV). Baijiu typically ranges 40–60% ABV; energy value of 235 kcal/100 g from the input record is consistent with approximately 47% ABV spirits and is retained as the best available estimate. Protein and fat are confirmed zero for distilled spirits. Carbohydrate is trace (0.1 g/100 g). Micronutrient values are trace or zero as reported for distilled spirits in USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: N/A — Baijiu contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable for distilled spirits
gi_source.source_match_notes: Distilled spirits contain negligible carbohydrates (0.1 g/100 g) and are not assigned a glycemic index in the University of Sydney GI Database. Pure ethanol does not raise blood glucose via the glycaemic mechanism. GI is set to 0 (numeric, as required by template) and gi_category to not_applicable to reflect that the concept of GI does not apply to this food. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0 due to negligible carbohydrate content. Note: although GI=0 is recorded here as a numeric placeholder per template requirements, this should not be interpreted as a measured low-GI value; the correct interpretation is that GI is not applicable for distilled spirits.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol raises blood pressure and antagonises antihypertensive medications
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Baijiu consumption, as regular alcohol intake is an independent risk factor for elevated blood pressure and can reduce the efficacy of antihypertensive drugs.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol causes unpredictable glycaemic fluctuations and hypoglycaemia risk with diabetes medications
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise extreme caution with Baijiu, as alcohol can cause hypoglycaemia especially when taken with insulin or sulfonylureas, and should never be consumed on an empty stomach.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High-ABV alcohol consumption increases cardiovascular risk including arrhythmia, cardiomyopathy, and stroke
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Baijiu, as its high alcohol content (35–60% ABV) is associated with increased risk of cardiac arrhythmia, hypertensive heart disease, and haemorrhagic stroke.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Alcohol is pro-inflammatory and interacts with RA medications including methotrexate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid excessive Baijiu consumption as alcohol can exacerbate systemic inflammation and interact adversely with disease-modifying drugs such as methotrexate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baijiu (白酒, literally 'white liquor'), also romanized as Báijiǔ and known by the alternate name shaojiu (烧酒), is a clear, potent distilled spirit of Chinese origin and one of the world's most consumed alcoholic beverages by volume. It is produced through solid-state fermentation of grains — most commonly sorghum, but also wheat, rice, barley, or millet depending on regional style — using Jiuqu, a complex starter culture of moulds, yeasts, and bacteria. The fermented mash is then distilled and aged in large clay or ceramic jars. Baijiu is characterized by its high alcohol content, typically 35–60% ABV, and its complex aromatic profiles classified into four main fragrance types: Strong (浓香, nóng xiāng), Light (清香, qīng xiāng), Sauce (酱香, jiàng xiāng, as in Moutai), and Rice (米香, mǐ xiāng). Nutritionally, Baijiu is essentially a pure alcohol beverage: it provides approximately 235 kcal per 100 g almost entirely from ethanol, with negligible protein (0 g), fat (0 g), and carbohydrate (0.1 g). It contains no meaningful vitamins or minerals. Clinically, Baijiu poses significant risks for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its high ethanol content. It is deeply embedded in Chinese social and ceremonial culture, traditionally served at banquets, business dinners, and festive celebrations in small thimble-sized glasses, with communal toasting (ganbei, 干杯) being a central ritual.
common_user_queries: What is the alcohol content of Baijiu?
Is Baijiu the same as rice wine?
Can people with high blood pressure drink Baijiu?
What grain is Baijiu made from?
What is the difference between Moutai and Baijiu?
meal_context: festive celebrations such as Chinese New Year

confidence_reason: Baijiu is not listed as a distinct entry in USDA FoodData Central or other approved primary sources. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy entry for distilled spirits (80 proof) and are consistent with the input record's energy value of 235 kcal/100 g, which corresponds to approximately 47% ABV. The adaptation is well-supported for a distilled spirit with negligible non-alcohol macronutrients, but the exact ABV of a generic Baijiu record introduces moderate uncertainty.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero: Baijiu is a distilled spirit; all protein from the grain mash is removed during distillation, confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for distilled spirits. fat_g is naturally zero: distilled spirits contain no fat by nature of the distillation process, confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. carbohydrate_g is trace (0.1 g/100 g): residual trace carbohydrate may remain in some Baijiu styles; value retained from input record and consistent with USDA data for distilled spirits. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are confirmed zero for distilled spirits per USDA FoodData Central. water_g set to -1: water content of Baijiu varies significantly with ABV (35–60%) and is not reported as a fixed value in USDA FoodData Central for generic distilled spirits; detailed moisture data unavailable for a generic Baijiu record as of 2026. Glycemic index is recorded as numeric 0 per template requirements; gi_category is set to not_applicable to reflect that the GI concept does not apply to distilled spirits with negligible carbohydrates (0.1 g/100 g); this value should not be interpreted as a measured low-GI result. Duplicate portion entry in input record has been deduplicated to a single 25 ml serving. Halal flag set to false as Baijiu is an alcoholic beverage. Gluten-free flag set to false as wheat-based Jiuqu is commonly used in production.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Baijiu', a precise canonical English name. Chinese-script local names (白酒, 烧酒) are present. Baijiu is a Chinese beverage; G6 requires Chinese-script coverage which is satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (235.0), protein_g (0.0), fat_g (0.0), carbohydrate_g (0.1) all present and numeric. Zero macros justified in notes with 'naturally zero' and 'confirmed zero' language. Source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy adapted for distilled spirits.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (1.0), saturated_fat_g (0.0), sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values and plausible for a distilled spirit.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric 0 satisfying the template requirement for a numeric value. gi_category set to 'not_applicable' to correctly convey that GI does not apply to distilled spirits. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source is documented referencing University of Sydney GI Database with full explanation. Notes field clarifies that the numeric 0 is a template placeholder and not a measured GI result.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn approximate match to FOODON_03301263 (distilled alcoholic beverage) documented with gap note. FoodEx2 approximate match to AN0GJ (Spirits) documented with gap note. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. No codes invented. Match types set to 'closest_parent'.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers what Baijiu is, production method, fragrance types, nutritional profile, clinical relevance, and cultural context. Suitable for RAG/LLM training.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correct), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: All strings in aliases_by_language verified present in flat aliases array: 白酒, 烧酒, Báijiǔ, Shāojiǔ, shaojiu, Baijiu, Chinese white liquor, Chinese spirit, Chinese distilled spirit, Chinese grain liquor all present. Aliases comprehensive covering English variants, Chinese script, and romanized forms.

Updated gi_source notes and record notes to clarify that 0 is a numeric template placeholder and gi_category 'not_applicable' conveys the correct clinical interpretation for a negligible-carbohydrate distilled spirit.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Baijiu record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for distilled spirits with documented adaptation. Zero macros justified with 'naturally zero' and 'confirmed zero' language. GI set to numeric 0 per template requirement with gi_category 'not_applicable' and full explanatory notes clarifying the placeholder interpretation. Ontology anchors are honest closest-parent matches with gap notes. All three mandatory health conditions present with specific clinical guidance. Aliases comprehensive and internally consistent. LLM description is detailed and RAG-ready.
